next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

Nate spent four days turning Eastbridge Plaza into flash cards.

He had the project file open on Dominic's desk, a yellow legal pad beside it, and three stacks of printouts he had no business pretending to understand: pages from the Connecticut Department of Environmental Protection website, a lender memo copied from somebody else's binder, and a zoning packet with so much highlighted on it the yellow stopped meaning anything. He'd written phrases in block letters to make them stick. PHASE II. REMEDIATION ESCROW. APPEAL WINDOW. CARRYING COSTS. He'd drawn arrows between them like the arrows would supply the logic.

This was not that different from how he'd survived tech support, if he was being honest. The system would break, he would search the knowledge base, skim five threads from people smarter than him, memorize a few nouns, and then say them back to the customer in a tone that implied sequence and control. Usually that worked because the person on the phone was some regional sales manager who could barely restart a printer without calling IT. Victor Kassos was not a guy with a printer problem. Victor had built the thing Nate was trying to jam a screwdriver into.

He knew that. He kept going anyway.

Late September had started to cool off, but the office never changed with the weather. The fluorescent lights flattened everything into the same season: carpet tile, beige partitions, the dry breath of the air-conditioning, desktop towers giving off steady heat under every desk. When Nate came in early the place felt less like a company than a server room with...
open_hooks_to_carry: Gail's diner: the turkey club, the gossip, the small-world texture of the strip mall community
The barber, the dry cleaner, the bodega — Nate as anonymous regular, a relief from the performance of being Dominic
prev_tail_excerpt:
.... Morton Lazar sat at the top of the page, Dominic Altieri sat at the bottom, and his father's death warrant lay in his own hand.

He had wanted the story of his life to be simple because simple stories were easier to live inside. A man ruined your family and you ruined him back. It felt clean enough to build a machine for and clean enough to stake nineteen years of resentment on. The file on the desk had none of that simplicity in it. It had dates and margins and routing slips and the kind of calm administrative violence that did not even require cruelty, only enough distance to call a store a parcel and a family business an existing use.

The fluorescent tube near reception buzzed harder, then one bank of lights shut off on its timer, dropping half the office into shadow. Nate did not move. The remaining lights cast a flat white rectangle over Dominic's desk, the file, the letter, his hands.

The date was December 28, 2005. It was five months away.

If he was going to stop the demolition, that was the deadline. If he was going to fail, that was the deadline too. The schedule did not care which version happened. It would keep moving until somebody with enough leverage jammed a bar into the gears, and Nate did not know yet where the gears actually were.

He sat there in the fluorescent buzz with the notice letter under his palm until the paper turned warm. Memory did not feel like that. History did not need utility shutoffs, fencing, and people assigned to return calls. This did. It was a live process with dates on it, and his own stolen hand was already inside the machine.
state_deltas_required: The gravitational pull of the strip mall community and Nate's growing attachment to people he knows are about to lose everything
Nate connects with the community and has his first real conversation with Mort. The demolition stakes shift from historical grievance to present, personal, specific loss.
